comments.text: On the video you're showing south region of Azerbaijan and talking about Northern regions of Azerbaijan, Lezgin people and Qusar district. The village you are showing and talking which is Vistan is not the village of Qusar and is not covered by Caucasus mountains. It's located in Lerik district and covered by Talysh mountains and those people are Talysh people not Lezgin. Just for your information

comments.author: @Aventurex-686
comments.text: The Caucasus villages almost feel like hidden pieces of another century quietly surviving above the clouds. One minute the scenery looks peaceful and timeless, the next minute you realize people have lived in these harsh mountains longer than many modern countries have existed. Honestly, that perspective feels humbling.

replies.author: @RemoteEarthJourneys
replies.text:
Beautifully said. I think that's exactly what makes places like these so captivating: they remind us that history isn't only preserved in museums, but in everyday routines, languages, and homes that have endured for centuries.

There's something profoundly humbling about realizing that while the modern world changes at incredible speed, entire communities in the Caucasus still wake up to the same mountains their ancestors knew hundreds of years ago. It makes you wonder—which will disappear first: these villages, or our ability to appreciate such a slower way of life?

replies.author: @manjurkadertalukder4891
replies.text:
Your glimpses on this documentary on just a fractional 
highlighting on the Caucasus people, life & typical hardships and the culture developed thereof are stunningly incredible and wondering. Their traditional stone housing, dairy herding, natural wool n fabric colouring, wood n copper work n crafting, 
and a vast varieties with so many more unseen, indeed seems as if we've witnessed another century being in this era!!!
The surroundings like Iranian Zagros also propagated with alike life style n cultures reminds us about the same stream of peoples' strugglesome mountainous survival. We've great honour for their heroic striving against this high altitudes 
nature & life dwellings.

replies.author: @PlanetVista4K
replies.text: I completely agree! There's something truly awe-inspiring about these ancient villages and the resilience of the people who call them home. It really puts things into perspective.

comments.author: @khaleemgul5307
comments.text: Beautiful country ❤️ 🎉

replies.author: @PlanetVista4K
replies.text: Glad you enjoyed the documentary! Azerbaijan truly is a beautiful country with so much to offer.

comments.author: @NILESHPATEL-v8h
comments.text: The point of reality is we think like this peoples living good life fresh air water etc. but ? they have lack of money and struglling withdaily life. and no educataion and no saving for future. .

replies.author: @PlanetVista4K
replies.text: That's a really profound observation and a beautiful perspective to share. It's inspiring to hear how your extensive travels have shaped your understanding of happiness.

replies.author: @sgtmaj5211
replies.text: I have travelled to more than 80 countries , and have learned that poor people are the happiest. Life is not about money, education, status, and other things people put focus on. The struggles in life is what makes great resilient people. With love and family, people can be happy in any environment. Not just speaking what I think but what I have seen.

replies.author: @PlanetVista4K
replies.text: I really appreciate you noticing the depth of the information!

replies.author: @PlanetVista4K
replies.text: You've hit on a really important aspect of what I try to show in my travel documentaries. It's easy to romanticize, but the reality for many is indeed a daily struggle, despite the beautiful surroundings.

replies.author: @Trollslayer_LA
replies.text: How do you know all this? I’m sure they’re better off and more educated than the average Indian villager. The USSR made sure people across its vast territories learned Russian to integrate, so they are not only educated in Russian but also their native tongue Azeri.

comments.author: @RelaxHistoryAI
comments.text: The villages in the Caucasus Mountains look so peaceful

replies.author: @PlanetVista4K
replies.text: The peaceful atmosphere and slow pace of life there are truly captivating. Would you enjoy living in a place like that?

comments.author: @נהוראי-ז9ה
comments.text: Love the storytelling here, not just pretty views but a glimpse into a culture that's barely touched by tourism.

replies.author: @PlanetVista4K
replies.text: So glad you enjoyed the storytelling and the deeper dive into the culture! That's exactly what I aimed for.

comments.author: @HynekWilliams
comments.text: This really highlights how tradition shapes everyday life in the Caucasus—so cinematic and immersive 😮

replies.author: @trueunrealglobe
replies.text: The emotional storytelling made every destination feel alive, personal, and deeply connected to the people living there every single day naturally.

replies.author: @PlanetVista4K
replies.text: Thanks for the suggestion! A food tour through remote mountain villages would definitely be unforgettable 🏔️

comments.author: @EmmaWalker-v8o
comments.text: Beautiful documentary—sincere, calm, and visually striking. It makes me want to plan a real mountain trek soon.

replies.author: @PlanetVista4K
replies.text: Thank you so much! Azerbaijan truly has some breathtaking mountain villages and trails. If you ever trek through the Caucasus, which season would you love to explore most? 😊

comments.author: @AmandaRamirez-i5v
comments.text: Watching this, you can almost hear the quiet of the valleys and the rhythm of daily life away from the city noise.

comments.author: @SebastiánRodriguez-c2v
comments.text: Stunning 4K footage of those forgotten villages—the stone houses and rugged mountains feel ancient and powerful. 🔥

replies.author: @PlanetVista4K
replies.text: Those old mountain villages carry so much history — glad the 4K did them justice. Thank you! 🔥
